Electrical Signature Analysis

One signal. Full drivetrain visibility.

An induction motor is a bidirectional energy converter. Every mechanical event in the drivetrain creates a force variation on the shaft that modulates the motor's current draw. ESA reads those modulations from the motor control cabinet. No sensors on the asset. No physical access required.

At Schiphol Airport, SAM4 detected 9 out of 9 developing faults — including bearing damage that the existing vibration programme missed. Read the case study →

The physics Signal science What it detects ESA vs vibration Legacy MCSA vs SAM4 Boundaries Validation

The science

How a motor's electrical signature reveals drivetrain condition

An induction motor is a bidirectional energy converter. Electrical energy flows in. Mechanical energy flows out. But the reverse is also true: every mechanical event in the rotating system (bearing wear, rotor imbalance, hydraulic turbulence, gear mesh) creates a force variation on the motor shaft. That force variation modulates the motor's current draw.

The current signal, measured at the Motor Control Centre, carries a complete record of the mechanical state of the entire drivetrain. The air gap between stator and rotor acts as a transducer. Shaft material is elastic. Bearings have clearances. Any mechanical disturbance causes the rotor to move within the air gap, modulating the magnetic field and producing measurable changes in the current and voltage waveforms.

Faults modulate the supply frequency like a radio carrier wave. A worn bearing creates periodic force variations at its characteristic frequency. These variations appear as sideband patterns around the supply frequency in the current spectrum. Each fault type produces a unique spectral fingerprint.

SAM4 reads those fingerprints continuously from the motor control cabinet. AI models trained on 10,000+ industrial assets compare each motor's signature against known fault patterns and its own baseline behaviour.

Motor Control Cabinet CT/VT clamps capture current and voltage
AI Models Trained on 10,000+ assets
Expert Validation Engineer reviews every anomaly
SAM4 Dashboard Validated alerts and recommended actions

The science is only half the story

ESA captures the signal. AI identifies the anomaly. But every alert is reviewed by a Samotics reliability engineer before it reaches your team. They confirm fault type, assess severity, and recommend a specific action. Time-critical alerts are escalated immediately.

See the full service model →

Signal science

How fault signatures appear in the current spectrum

Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) converts raw current and voltage waveforms from the time domain into frequency-domain spectra. This reveals the harmonic content that characterises fault signatures. Each fault type creates distinct patterns in specific frequency bands.

Broken rotor bars

f1(1 ± 2s)

Broken or cracked rotor bars produce sidebands at f1(1 ± 2s), where f1 is the supply frequency and s is the motor slip. For a 50 Hz motor running at 2% slip, the sidebands appear at 48 Hz and 52 Hz. Additional sidebands at f1(1 ± 2ks) for k = 1, 2, 3.

Bearing faults

BPFO · BPFI · BSF · FTF

Bearing defects generate amplitude modulations at characteristic frequencies: ball pass outer race (BPFO), ball pass inner race (BPFI), ball spin frequency (BSF), and fundamental train frequency (FTF). These appear as sidebands around the supply frequency in the current spectrum.

Eccentricity

frotor slots ± n × fr

Static and dynamic air gap eccentricity produce frequency components at the rotor slot harmonic frequency ± multiples of running speed. An uneven air gap alters the magnetic flux distribution, creating detectable current modulations.

Cavitation

Broadband noise elevation

Hydraulic turbulence from cavitation raises the broadband noise floor in the current spectrum, particularly around the supply frequency. Unlike discrete faults, cavitation produces a diffuse energy increase across multiple frequency bands.

How baselines work

SAM4 establishes a spectral baseline during an initial calibration period. This baseline captures the "healthy" spectrum for the specific motor, load, and operating conditions. Ongoing monitoring compares live spectra against this baseline. Deviations in specific frequency bands flag developing faults before mechanical failure occurs.

Detection capability

What ESA detects across the drivetrain

ESA detects faults across the full drivetrain — weeks to months before failure. The matrix below maps fault types to equipment categories, with detection confidence based on over 10,000 monitored assets.

Fault type AC Motors Pumps Fans Gearboxes Compressors
Bearing degradation ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓
Misalignment ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓ ✓
Imbalance ✓ ✓ ✓ ● ✓
Cavitation — ✓ — — —
Clogging / blockage — ✓ ● — —
Electrical faults ✓ ✓ ✓ — ✓
Process deviations ● ✓ ● ● ✓
Efficiency degradation ✓ ✓ ✓ ● ●
✓ Full detection ● Partial / emerging — Not applicable

This matrix is non-exhaustive. ESA can detect additional fault types depending on asset configuration and operating conditions.

See customer results →

Why ESA

What ESA sees that vibration cannot

ESA and vibration analysis are complementary technologies. But ESA covers three gaps that vibration sensors leave open.

Electrical faults are invisible to vibration

Vibration sensors cannot detect power quality issues, winding insulation degradation, or rotor bar defects. ESA detects both electrical and mechanical faults from a single measurement point. For a reliability engineer, that means one monitoring system covers the full fault spectrum.

No F=ma limitation

Vibration detection depends on Newton's second law: F = ma. Larger machines need more force to generate a detectable acceleration signal. This masks early-stage faults on heavy assets. ESA reads current modulation directly, independent of machine mass and stiffness.

Voltage discriminates fault origin

ESA uses both voltage and current signals. This distinguishes whether an anomaly originates from the power supply or from within the motor system. MCSA (current only) cannot make this distinction. SAM4 analyses both, eliminating false diagnoses caused by supply-side disturbances.

See the full ESA vs vibration comparison →

What's different

Same physics. Different experience.

Motor Current Signature Analysis has existed for over 40 years. It works — but it requires a specialist, a scheduled visit, and manual interpretation. SAM4 turns ESA into a continuous, automated service.

Legacy MCSA

  • Monitoring mode Periodic — scheduled visits
  • Operator Specialist with oscilloscope
  • Data interpretation Manual expert analysis
  • Scale One asset at a time
  • Output Report (days–weeks later)
  • Fleet coverage Typically 5–20 critical assets

SAM4 ESA

  • Monitoring mode Continuous — 24/7 automated
  • Operator No specialist required
  • Data interpretation AI models + diagnostic team
  • Scale Entire fleet simultaneously
  • Output Real-time alerts + dashboard
  • Fleet coverage 1,000+ assets per site

The physics hasn't changed. The experience has — from a specialist tool to an operational platform your maintenance team can use.

See how ESA compares to vibration monitoring →

Honest boundaries

Where ESA works best. And where it doesn't.

Best suited for

  • Submerged, enclosed, or hazardous-area assets
  • Fleet-wide monitoring from a single MCC
  • Continuous 24/7 surveillance without downtime
  • Electrical fault detection (insulation, phase imbalance, rotor bars)
  • Assets where vibration sensors are impractical or impossible
  • Full drivetrain visibility: motor, coupling, gearbox, driven load

Constraints to know

  • VFD-driven motors: Inverter switching noise reduces resolution for bearing fault frequencies and rotor bar sidebands. SAM4 compensates by analysing VFD input signals and applying adaptive filtering. Mechanical faults (unbalance, misalignment) remain fully detectable. Bearing detection accuracy is reduced but not eliminated.
  • Minimum RPM: Typically 300+ RPM for reliable detection. High-slip motors (cranes, hoists) may need longer observation windows.
  • MCC access: Physical access to the motor control cabinet is required for clamp installation. Installation takes under 60 minutes per asset.
  • Calibration period: 2 days to 2 weeks before full detection capability, depending on load variability.
  • Three-phase only: Single-phase motors are not supported.

Validation

From IEEE papers to 10,000 monitored assets

40+ years of ESA science
10,000+ assets monitored globally
>90% of developing faults detected
80+ customers across 5 continents

Oak Ridge National Laboratory

ESA was developed at Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) in 1985 for monitoring motor-operated valves in nuclear power plant safety systems. US Patent 4965513 (1989). The technology has been refined and deployed across heavy industry for four decades.

ISO 20958:2013

The international standard for condition monitoring and diagnostics using electrical signature analysis of three-phase induction motors. SAM4's methodology aligns with ISO 20958.

ABB integration

ABB evaluated every ESA approach on the market and chose Samotics. SAM4 ESA is integrated into ABB's ACS880 drives. ABB holds a 10% equity stake.

Peer-reviewed research

ESA methods published in IEEE, EPRI, and leading journals since the 1980s. Thomson's 50 industrial case histories. Penrose's motor diagnostics training programme. The physics is established and independently validated.

“Instead of needing to be placed on the asset in the field, SAM4 installs inside the motor control cabinet, out of harm's way. And its advanced analytics were just what we needed.”

Andy Roegis Industrial Digitalization Manager, ArcelorMittal
